name: Discovery Sprint
bestFor: A business that knows something is wrong but cannot yet describe the requirement in a way anyone can quote.
shape: A short, bounded engagement: sessions with the people who do the work, a review of the existing systems, and a written findings pack.
whatDivyamDoes: Runs discovery sessions with each stakeholder group
Reviews existing tools, spreadsheets and workarounds
Separates the urgent problem from the eventual ambition
Writes the open questions no one has answered yet
Produces a discovery summary and a solution direction
whatTheClientDoes: Provides access to the people who actually run the process
Provides read access to current systems
Nominates a decision-maker who can resolve open questions

name: Process & Architecture Engagement
bestFor: A business that has agreed the problem and needs a future-state process plus the system design to support it.
shape: Current-state mapping, future-state redesign, application and data architecture, then a documented scope that can be estimated.
whatDivyamDoes: Maps the current state with the people who run it
Designs the future state and its control points
Selects applications and defines the data model
Separates standard functionality from custom work
Documents scope, assumptions, dependencies and risks
whatTheClientDoes: Confirms which systems remain the source of truth

name: Documentation Engagement
bestFor: A decision already taken that now needs to survive a handover to an implementation team.
shape: Existing material in, implementation-ready document out.
whatDivyamDoes: Reconciles calls, notes, screenshots and prior documents into one structure
Resolves contradictions between sources
Writes the ISO, FRD or TRD
Marks every unresolved point as an open decision rather than an assumption

name: Delivery Oversight
bestFor: A build that is underway and drifting from the requirement it was scoped against.
shape: Ongoing involvement across the build: requirement clarification, output review, testing structure and UAT.
whatDivyamDoes: Clarifies requirements for the delivery team
Reviews build output against documented acceptance criteria
Identifies hidden scope and change requests early
Structures testing and UAT
Prepares go-live and adoption planning
whatTheClientDoes: Makes business users available for UAT

name: Partner / White-Label Support
bestFor: An implementation partner who needs discovery, architecture or client-facing documentation produced under their own brand.
shape: I work behind the partner. The end client sees the partner's brand, the partner's tone and the partner's document template.
whatDivyamDoes: Supports or runs the discovery call
Produces the solution direction and architecture
Writes client-facing documentation in the partner's template
Supports estimation and scope protection
Keeps internal reasoning out of client-facing material
whatTheClientDoes: Supplies the brief, brand assets and commercial constraints
Owns the client relationship and the commercial conversation

name: System Review
bestFor: A CRM or operational system that was implemented, went live, and is not being used.
shape: A structured review of the configuration against the process it was meant to serve.
whatDivyamDoes: Reviews configuration, roles, automation and reporting
Compares the built system against the real process
Interviews users about the workarounds they have invented
Prioritises fixes by effort and business effect
whatTheClientDoes: Accepts findings that may be uncomfortable
